text: While porous metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have long been recognized for their chemical versatility, their integration into electronic devices has been hindered by typically low electrical conductivity. In this work, we demonstrate that a family of conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs can serve as an effective active layer for chemiresistive sensing. By utilizing a series of structurally analogous but chemically distinct frameworks—incorporating different metal centers and organic linkers—we developed a cross-reactive sensor array capable of detecting volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Through statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PCA) and linear discriminant analysis (LDA), the array successfully differentiates between various functional group classes, including alcohols, ketones, amines, and hydrocarbons, with over 90% accuracy. Mechanistic investigations suggest that the sensing response originates from multiple, simultaneous processes, including charge transfer and hydrogen bonding, which provide a degree of orthogonality not typically found in traditional chemiresistive materials. These findings establish conductive 2D MOFs as a tunable and robust platform for the development of high-performance, multifunctional vapor sensors.
